coredata.dc:title: Insights into the photoluminescence properties of gel-like carbon quantum dots embedded in poly(methyl methacrylate) polymer
coredata.dc:creator: Rodrigues, Joana
Pereira, Sónia O.
Soreto Teixeira, S.
Zhou, Yiqun
Peng, Zhili
Liyanage, Piumi Y.
Leblanc, Roger M.
Barros-Timmons, Ana M.M.V.
Costa, Luis C.
Costa, Florinda M.
Monteiro, Teresa
coredata.dc:description: Carbon dots (CDs) have become one of the most investigated nanomaterials in the last few years, since they offer excellent prospects to be used in a wide range of technological applications. Nonetheless, the understanding of the photoluminescence (PL) processes remains unclear and solely based on spectroscopic measurements at room temperature, which hampered a full discussion on the nature of recombination mechanisms. Insights into the CDs’ PL and corresponding recombination models can be achieved from temperature, excitation energy and excitation density dependency studies. Thus, in this paper, CDs with a mean diameter of ∼3.0 nm were embedded in poly(methyl methacrylate) (PMMA) and studied by such optical techniques. The recombination of CDs/PMMA composites was found to be due to an overlap of emitting centres, both in the blue and green regions, with intensity and peak position strongly dependent on the excitation density. Moreover, this emission was found to be preferentially populated by two overlapped excitation bands. Temperature-dependent PL measurements corroborate the hypothesis of overlapped emitting optical centres (e.g. small molecules at the CDs’ surface), as no shift on peak position and no changes in spectral shape were observed, which allow to exclude the hypothesis of a conventional free excitonic transition and quantum confinement effects, as observed in typical nanosized semiconductors with dimensions lower than the exciton Bohr radius.
coredata.dcterms:subject: CDs/PMMA
Photoluminescence
Photoluminescence excitation
Excitation density dependence
Excitation energy dependence
Temperature dependence

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Preparation of PMMA films containing CDs

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a), (b) HRTEM images of the CDs. (c) CDs mean size distribution. (d) Magnified HRTEM showing the estimated interplanar distance.
RT transmittance of the CDs/PMMA composites with different CDs’ loads. For comparison, the spectrum of the polymer matrix is also presented.

(a) RT PL spectra of the CDs/PMMA composites with different CDs’ loads and excited with 325 nm photons (either from a Xe lamp or a He-Cd laser). (b) PL spectra showing the absolute intensity for all the composite samples and (c) comparison of the PL spectra for the composite with 3% of CDs, unloaded PMMA matrix and CDs in powder form (all obtained with He-Cd laser excitation).
(a) PLE spectra of the CDs/PMMA composites loaded with 1.5%, 3% and 5% CDs and monitored at 445 nm and 520 nm. (b) Representative PL and PLE spectra for the sample loaded with 3% CDs.

(a) Temperature-dependent PL spectra of the CDs/PMMA composite loaded with 3% of CDs, obtained with 325 nm laser line excitation. (b) Same spectra shown in (a) normalised to the band maxima to clearly demonstrate the absence of any significant energy shift with increasing temperature.

head.ce:title: Insights into the photoluminescence properties of gel-like carbon quantum dots embedded in poly(methyl methacrylate) polymer

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Physics Department & I3N, Aveiro University, Campus Universitário de Santiago, 3810-193 Aveiro, Portugal

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemistry, University of Miami, 1301 Memorial Drive, Coral Gables, Florida, 33146, USA

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: CICECO – Aveiro Institute of Materials and Department of Chemistry, Aveiro University, Campus Universitário de Santiago, 3810-193 Aveiro, Portugal

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: CICECO – Aveiro Institute of Materials and Department of Chemistry

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Carbon dots (CDs) have become one of the most investigated nanomaterials in the last few years, since they offer excellent prospects to be used in a wide range of technological applications. Nonetheless, the understanding of the photoluminescence (PL) processes remains unclear and solely based on spectroscopic measurements at room temperature, which hampered a full discussion on the nature of recombination mechanisms. Insights into the CDs’ PL and corresponding recombination models can be achieved from temperature, excitation energy and excitation density dependency studies. Thus, in this paper, CDs with a mean diameter of ∼3.0 nm were embedded in poly(methyl methacrylate) (PMMA) and studied by such optical techniques. The recombination of CDs/PMMA composites was found to be due to an overlap of emitting centres, both in the blue and green regions, with intensity and peak position strongly dependent on the excitation density. Moreover, this emission was found to be preferentially populated by two overlapped excitation bands. Temperature-dependent PL measurements corroborate the hypothesis of overlapped emitting optical centres (e.g. small molecules at the CDs’ surface), as no shift on peak position and no changes in spectral shape were observed, which allow to exclude the hypothesis of a conventional free excitonic transition and quantum confinement effects, as observed in typical nanosized semiconductors with dimensions lower than the exciton Bohr radius.

ce:section.ce:para: As a new class of enhanced fluorescent nanomaterials, carbon quantum dots (CDs) have attracted considerable interest by the scientific community as promising candidates to replace the toxic metal-based semiconductor quantum dots [1], owing to their potentialities for a wide range of applications, namely in fields of optoelectronics, biomedicine, energy and sensors [2–17]. For instance, some authors reported that the CDs’ emission could be pumped via a multiple photon absorption process through upconversion phenomena, which has particular importance for bio and energy-related applications, since this process enables the use of low energy near-infrared (NIR) photons [13,15,18,19]. Considering, as an example, the use of these materials as biomarkers, the possibility of using wavelengths within the biological optical window (from ∼650 to 1000 nm) results in less absorption by the biological medium. It allows the radiation to penetrate more deeply in the tissues (when compared with light with shorter wavelengths), which promotes a more efficient excitation and a lower signal-to-noise ratio [20]. This fact, associated with the chemical inertness of the CDs and combined with their low toxicity, constitutes the driving force for the development of versatile biocompatible fluorescent multifunctional platforms, suitable for both in-vitro and in-vivo optical imaging [5,11,13–15,18,21–25]. In turn, some authors have reported that the CDs’ photoluminescence (PL) intensity can be quenched in the presence of donors/acceptors and metallic species [5,11,25–28]. This quenching mechanism has been explained by photoinduced redox processes [26,27] and on/off optical switch of the CDs emission [5,14,25,29–31], which have particular importance for applications in photocatalysis and sensing.

Typically, CDs exhibit an almost spherical shape, with diameters up to 10 nm [2,12,32–34]. They can be either amorphous or crystalline [1,2,14,35,36], depending on the preparation methodology and the precursors used. In fact, a myriad of strategies has been employed in the production of such nanomaterials, either by top-down or bottom-up approaches, resulting in distinctive material’s characteristics [4,17,23,37–39]. Indeed, the reaction media/precursors are known to dramatically affect the formation of CDs and may result in different doping, size and surface functionalisation, leading to distinct structural and optical properties that are dependent on their electronic and surface states [35].

Upon organic/inorganic surface passivation, these materials often exhibit tuneable emission under ultraviolet/blue excitation, ranging from the visible to the NIR [19,21,38–43], which is usually characterised by a broad band when probed at room temperature (RT). As a result of the dissimilar structural and chemical properties conferred by the synthesis methods, the PL features are often quite different for the various types of CDs. In fact, besides the peak position, the spectral shape and full width at half maximum (FWHM) can differ. Additionally, the main maximum of the visible band can either be sensitive [5,15,17,32,33] or nearly insensitive [13,30,32–34] to the used excitation energy. Despite all the miscellany of properties evidenced by the countless CDs reported in the literature, surprisingly they possess similar spectroscopic features, which may be a reflection of common photophysical processes occurring in the systems [44,45]. Generally, the emission of CDs is mostly attributed to the surface groups rather than the size, and the most conventional CDs have strong PL from blue to green [45].

It has been reported that the CDs emission is mainly due to surface mediated processes involving the radiative recombination of photoinduced carriers trapped at surface defects (e.g. surface functional groups) [14,17,18,27,46–48], with decay times in the nanosecond timescale [2,11,12,34,37,41,49]. However, other explanations for the luminescence nature of these CDs have been proposed. For instance, Zhu et al. [45] published a review article in 2015 claiming that at least four PL mechanisms have been discussed for different types of fluorescent CDs: (i) conjugated π-domains or quantum confinement effect, being driven by the carbon core; (ii) surface states, attributed to the hybridisation of the carbon backbone and connected chemical groups; (iii) fluorescent molecules connected on the CDs’ surface or carbon core; and (iv) crosslink-enhanced emission effect [45]. Other reports also indicate that the main emission can be explained by the presence of self-trapped excitons in a network of polycyclic aromatic hydrocarbons [42] or oxidised carbon groups [50]. This lack of consensus in the scientific community regarding the origin of the CDs’ luminescence has motivated intense research in the field, with new proposals and theories arising everyday [8,34,35,44,45,51–53]. Nevertheless, the nature of the CDs’ surface seems to be the common denominator of interpretations.

Recently, Chen et al. [35] reported a work where a top-down method was used to explore the feasibility of preparing similar CDs using different solvents. To attenuate the solvent effect, the carbon materials were oxidised in a controlled way, using diluted nitric acid (1 M) prior to the solvothermal/hydrothermal treatment. The authors observed that the produced CDs exhibited similar structure and dimensions. Even so, differences in the peak position of the broad band maximum were detected depending on the solvent used (water, dimethylformamide (DMF) and methanol) and excitation energy. Furthermore, by removing the CDs from the original solvent and placing them in water led to additional changes in the PL signal, which corroborates the strong dependence of the optical properties on the surface characteristics. Also, Wang et al. [34] demonstrated that CDs synthesised from p-phenylenediamine could emit from the green to the red spectral region (in this case independent of the excitation energy) when dispersed in different solvents, evidencing that the CDs’ emission can be tuned by an appropriate choice of solvent, i.e. surface media. The authors attributed this dependence to a dipole induced surface electronic state change. Additionally, they prepared CDs/polymer composites where the polymers were chosen in order to possess chemical groups similar to the ones present in the tested solvents. This resulted again in a strong PL emission, ranging from dark green to red, depending on the chosen polymer matrix, but with no further shift in the band peak position resulting from the excitation wavelength. Papaioannou et al. [1] examined light absorption and emission in glucose-derived CDs prepared by hydrothermal carbonisation and found a complex excitation-dependent light emission for samples suspended in solvents, which was seen to be affected by the polarity of the solvent used to prepare the colloidal suspension. More interestingly, these authors found that solvent-free samples exhibited drastic differences in their light emission, demonstrating that, in such case, no excitation-dependent PL was observed. They claimed that both solvent polarity and pH were likely to have a strong effect on the surface-related emission. The effect of the pH on the luminescent emission has also been reported by several other authors [5,24,30,32,38,40,41].

In view of what has been outlined above, the incorporation of CDs into solid matrixes, like is the case of polymers, requires additional optical characterisation, since it is expected that the emission features of the CDs may be affected when compared to the ones obtained for the colloidal suspension and/or powdered CDs. Such composites are of particular importance when applications like bright emitters for photonics and/or optoelectronic devices are envisaged, conferring properties as high transparency, flexibility, mechanical strength and cost effectiveness to the composites [17].

Although great advances have already been achieved regarding the processing and characterisation of this type of CDs and corresponding polymer based composites, the heterogeneity of the samples prepared and their tuneable surface chemical groups remain a challenge for a proper understanding of their properties. As such, it is not trivial to establish a PL recombination mechanism able to describe the phenomena that are observed for the different samples. Furthermore, most of the reported spectroscopic studies have been carried out at RT in different aqueous and organic media. Though relevant for the point of view of applications, such analysis is not enough for a full comprehension of CDs’ recombination processes. Thus, in order to gain further insight regarding the mechanisms involved in the CDs PL emission, temperature-dependent (11 K to RT) steady-state PL measurements were conducted for the first time in CDs embedded in poly(methyl methacrylate) (CDs/PMMA). Excitation density-dependent PL and PL excitation (PLE) analyses were also performed to assess the optical properties of the CDs/PMMA composites. The combination of these results allowed to exclude the hypothesis of a typical semiconductor free exciton recombination nature, suggesting that the luminescence signal is due to an overlap of emitting centres, likely related to the presence of small fluorescent molecules at the CDs’ surface.

Citric acid (∼99.5–100%), acetone (99.9%) and tetrahydrofuran (THF, 99.9% Analar NORMAPUR®) were purchased from VWR and 1,2-ethylenediamine (EDA) from MP Biomedicals. Compressed air with ultra-high purity was acquired from Airgas. Preparative silica gel TLC plates (C18, 20 × 20 cm2) and poly(methyl methacrylate) (PMMA, Mw 350 000) were purchased from Sigma-Aldrich. The filtration was performed through a 25 mL syringe filter with 0.2 mm PTFE membrane purchased from VWR. Deionized water was obtained from a Modulab 2020 water purification system at RT (20.0 ± 0.5 °C) with a resistivity of 18 MΩ cm and surface tension of 72.6 mN.m−1.

Gel-like CDs were synthesised using citric acid and EDA as precursors, as described by Y. Zhou et al. [33]. In brief, argon gas was used to drive O2 from solvothermal system for 5 min. Then, EDA (5 mL) was transferred into a 50 mL round-bottomed flask and heated under constant stirring in an oil bath using a hotplate (Chemglass, OptiMag-st). When the temperature reached 160 °C, citric acid (1 g) was quickly added under vigorous stirring and the reaction proceeded for 50 min until all the citric acid had completely reacted with EDA. After cooling the system for 15 min to RT, the CDs were deposited at the bottom of the flask. The remaining supernatant EDA was then rinsed out with acetone and the resultant CDs (0.8 g) were dissolved in water (1 mL) and heated using a rotary evaporator (Buchi, R-114) at a constant temperature between 70 and 80 °C (under reduced pressure) to completely evaporate the water. The CDs would then reform in this fashion.

An additional purification step was performed as reported in [54]. In short, 30 mL of acetone was added to 1 g of the as-prepared CDs to remove the residual EDA within the CDs by sonication and mixing by a digital Vortex mixer until the CDs changed from transparent to opaquely white. The precipitate collected was further purified by reversed-phase preparative silica gel TLC plate (C18), using a mixture of water and acetonitrile (7:3; va/vw). 100 mg of the CDs was dispersed into 500 mL of water and 40 mL of the developing solvent were added to the chromatographic jar. After 10 min, until the vapour-liquid equilibrium was reached, the TLC plate loaded with CDs was added into the jar to initiate the preparative TLC. After 90 min of elution, 4 bright distinct separate fractions were visible under the UV excitation light (365 nm). Due to the highest quantum yield of fraction 2 (55%) among all the CDs fractions, this fraction was chosen to be scraped off upon drying, together with the silica gel, and dispersed in water. After centrifuging using a Beckman Coulter centrifuge at a speed of 9000 rpm for 20 min to remove most of the silica, filtration was performed using three separate filters to completely remove the silica. Then, TLC was performed on fraction 2 again to ensure that it was well isolated. Finally, an aqueous solution of this repurified fraction was prepared, which was lyophilized yielding a yellowish powder.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of PMMA films containing CDs
ce:section.ce:para: For the preparation of CDs/PMMA composites, a stock dispersion of the above mentioned CDs powder was prepared in THF (2.76 mg.mL−1). Defined volumes of this dispersion were added to 2.5 mL of a solution of PMMA (92 mg.mL−1) in THF to obtain concentrations of 0.5%, 1.5%, 3.0% and 5% (% w/w). The mixtures were first dispersed in an ultrasonic bath for 20 min (40 °C) and then submitted to magnetic stirring for 2 h (at RT). To ensure complete homogenisation, the CDs/PMMA mixture in THF was placed again in the ultrasonic bath for an additional hour (at 40 °C). Then, 5 mL of each composite’s dispersion and a blank control (PMMA 46 mg.mL−1) were poured into a Teflon mould (3 × 3 cm2). The solvent evaporation was allowed to take place slowly during circa 2 days at RT in a desiccator with a small opening, affording the PMMA films containing the CDs.

Transmission measurements were performed at RT in a UV/VIS GBC Cintra 303 spectrometer in the range of 190–900 nm, using deuterium and tungsten lamps as excitation sources. Steady-state PL measurements were carried out using the 325 nm (3.8 eV) line from a cw He-Cd laser (Kimmon IK Series) as the excitation source. This laser line exhibits a beam spot of ∼1 mm and an excitation power density less than 0.6 W.cm−2. The luminescence was measured using a dispersive system SPEX 1704 monochromator (1 m, 1200 grooves.mm−1) fitted with a cooled Hamamatsu R928 photomultiplier tube. Excitation density-dependent PL studies were conducted at RT using the 325 nm He–Cd laser line and a set of neutral density filters. The same laser line was used as the excitation source to measure the temperature-dependent PL spectra. In the latter experiments, the samples were mounted in a cold finger of a closed-cycle helium cryostat and the temperature was controlled in the range of 11 K to RT. The spectra were corrected to the spectral response of the optical components, detector and excitation source. Additionally, the excitation energy dependence of the PL was investigated at RT using a Xe lamp coupled to a monochromator in a Fluorolog-3 Horiba Scientific modular equipment double additive grating scanning monochromator (2 × 180 mm, 1200 grooves·mm−1) in the excitation and a triple grating iHR550 spectrometer in the emission (550 mm, 1200 grooves·mm−1). RT PLE was recorded in the same system by monitoring at the energy corresponding to the maxima of the PL emission bands and the excitation was scanned to higher energies.

The morphological and structural properties of the CDs were evaluated by high resolution transmission electron microscopy (HRTEM) using a JEOL 2200FS operated at 200 kV and equipped with an Oxford (INCA Energy TEM 250) energy dispersive X-ray spectroscopy (EDS) detector. The sample was prepared by placing a drop of diluted aqueous colloidal solution on a holey carbon film copper grid and left to evaporate in air.

Fig. 1 a) and b) show HRTEM images of the CDs. The CDs present an average diameter of ∼3.0 nm (Fig. 1c) and exhibit characteristic lattices fringes with in-plane interplanar spacing of 0.20 ± 0.02 nm (Fig. 1d), in line with the reported diffracting planes corresponding to the lattice periodicity of graphitic carbon [2,4,5,14,25,34,41,49]. Elemental analysis performed on the CDs by energy dispersive X-ray spectroscopy indicated the presence of Ca, Si, Na and K elements, possibly associated with the purification procedure (data not shown).

Fig. 2 shows the RT transmission spectra of the CDs/PMMA composites with different CDs’ loads. For comparison, a spectrum of the PMMA reference sample is also presented. The composites are highly transparent for wavelengths longer than 500 nm. At shorter wavelengths, two main absorption bands with maxima at ∼240 and ∼275 nm are observed. As they are also detected in the unload PMMA matrix, they are attributed to the absorption to carbonyl groups (CO), in agreement with the previously reported works of Sayyah et al. [55] and Ennis et al. [56], corresponding to the π →
π *
and n →
π *
transitions for the shorter and longer wavelengths, respectively. It is worth to note that a noticeable absorption shoulder between 310–600 nm (maxima ∼370 nm) is seen to develop with increasing CDs’ loading in the composites’ samples. Curiously, the absorption intensity increases up to 1.5%–3.0%, which is followed by a decrease for higher CDs contents, in line with the PL results showed below.

The RT PL spectra of the CDs/PMMA composites are shown in Fig. 3 . By exciting the samples with 325 nm, with lower and higher excitation densities (using a Xe lamp and a He-Cd laser, respectively), the main recombination processes result in a wide unstructured PL emission covering the ultraviolet and visible spectral regions. However, significant changes in the spectral shape and peak position are detected when the PL spectra are measured under the two distinct excitation conditions (Fig. 3a). When the lowest excitation density was used, the emission is peaked in the blue spectral region (∼ 445 nm), showing two main shoulders at shorter (∼390 nm) and longer wavelengths (∼520 nm). In turn, higher excitation density favours a shift of the PL maxima to longer wavelengths, denoting a larger contribution of the green component. Such process was found to be reversible upon ceasing the laser excitation, which suggests a possible influence of the samples’ local temperature in PMMA conformation, promoted by the highly focussed excitation source. The spectra in Fig. 3b), acquired with laser excitation (higher excitation density), show that the CDs/PMMA PL intensity increases with CDs’ load up to 3%, implying a correlation between the green emission band and the CDs content. The decrease in the intensity verified for the sample with 5% loading suggests that there is an optimum CDs concentration to maximize the luminescence output. Concentrations above 3% may result in a higher CDs aggregation, reducing the surface area of the CDs, which is believed to be responsible for the luminescent signal, as will be discussed below. In order to evaluate the influence of the polymeric host on the emission processes, Fig. 3c) shows a comparison between the normalized PL spectra of unloaded PMMA, CDs in powder form and CDs/PMMA composite (3%), obtained under the same excitation and alignment conditions. At RT and with the used ultraviolet excitation at the n →
π *
absorption of the carbonyl groups, the PMMA matrix exhibits a wide emission band peaked ∼390 nm, extending into the visible spectral region. The PL of the CDs covers an extremely wide spectral range with the maximum in the blue/green region, while the emission of CDs/PMMA composite can be discussed on the basis of an overlapping of emission bands arising from both the CDs and the PMMA polymeric host.

Fig. 4 shows the PL and PLE spectra of the CDs/PMMA composites obtained under low excitation conditions. In this case, the observed luminescence maxima evidenced limited sensitivity to the excitation energy, being however strongly dependent on excitation density, as shown in Fig. 3a). In particular, for low excitation density conditions and excitation wavelengths varying from 325 nm (3.8 eV) to 410 nm (3.0 eV), the PL band maxima shift to longer wavelengths only by ∼15 nm, from ∼ 440 to ∼ 455 nm. These results are in accordance with the ones reported by Wang et al. [34], where a nearly independence of the CDs’ PL peak position with excitation wavelength was also observed, either when dispersed in distinct solvents or in different polymeric matrixes. This also agrees with the recently reported work of Papaioannou et al. [1]. These authors found that, for solvent-free CDs, no excitation-dependent light emission was observed and concluded that the vast majority of the excitation-dependent PL reported to date, for CDs prepared by hydrothermal carbonisation, could be due to particle-solvent interactions rather than to the CDs themselves.

Independently of the CDs’ load, the PLE spectra of the CDs/PMMA samples displayed in Fig. 4a) and b) demonstrate a preferential population path of the samples’ emission corresponding to a well-defined excitation band peaked at 370 nm. The energy location of the excitation band agrees well with the absorption shoulder observed for the CDs/PMMA composites (Fig. 2), corroborating the previous assumption that embedding the CDs in the PMMA results in an additional absorption and a well-defined excitation band. On the other hand, it is worth to note that, when the PLE is monitored at the longer wavelengths, a new excitation band, at ∼410 nm, was detected, as seen for the case of the PLE monitored at ∼493 nm and 520 nm (Fig. 4b). Such observation suggests that, besides the deep ultraviolet excitation, two main excitation bands, with maxima at ∼370 nm and ∼410 nm, are responsible for the blue/green luminescence detected for the CDs/PMMA composites. Therefore, a likely explanation for the observed PL features can be proposed considering two (or more) emitting centres which are partially overlapped in the blue/green region. Moreover, the overlapping of their main excitation bands (∼370 nm for the blue emission and ∼410 nm for the green light) allows us to raise the hypothesis that the shifts in the PL peak position reported in the literature could be related to the preferential population of the two (or more) emitting centres when the excitation wavelength is varied. This leads to different contributions of the optical centres depending on the excitation conditions. This interpretation is in line with the recent explanation of the CDs luminescence provided by Fu et al. [42] and Santos et al. [19], where the role of small polycyclic aromatic hydrocarbon molecules at the CDs’ surface was discussed taking into account their typical absorption and emission spectra [42,57]. For instance, Fu et al. [42] were able to reproduce the optical properties of the CDs by considering the main absorption of anthracene, pyrene and perylene.

Additionally, the role of the PMMA on the observed luminescence should be discussed. On the one hand, interactions between the surface of CDs and the polymers are known to prevent aggregation and, therefore, contribute to higher PL intensity [43,51,58]. Such interactions may be dipole-dipole interactions, mainly through the carbonyl groups of PMMA, or even hydrogen bonds between functional groups at the surface of the CDs and the carbonyl groups of the polymeric host. On the other hand, the formation of films results in more confined environments around the CDs and, according to Liu et al. [58], film preparation can even be associated with a compression of the functional groups on the surface of CDs and an enhancement of the surface passivation effect, resulting in brighter CDs’ fluorescence. Furthermore, our data clearly evidence that the host polymer may act as a luminescence sensitizer. Indeed, by exciting the carbonyl groups of the PMMA, the emission of the polymer matrix (Fig. 3c)) partially overlaps with the main excitation bands (Fig. 4) of the CDs’ luminescence, meaning that energy transfer between the polymer and the CDs’ surface cannot be discarded to account for the PL enhanced properties.

The green emission from the CDs/PMMA observed with 325 nm laser excitation was studied as a function of temperature between 11 K and RT, as shown in Fig. 5 . The sample loaded with 3% CDs was chosen due to its highest PL intensity. No changes in the spectral shape or peak position of the emission were identified by heating the sample up to RT. The absence of a lower energy shift in the emission maxima supports the assumption that the emission detected in these CDs embedded in PMMA host does not behave as a free exciton recombination, contrary to what is typically observed in conventional semiconductor quantum dots. Indeed, if this was the case, a low energy shift corresponding to the bandgap shrinkage would be observed. Therefore, the temperature-dependent PL measurements support the hypothesis that the blue/green bands are likely due to an overlapping of optically active centres (e.g. small molecules on the CDs’ surface) present in the composite, which are preferentially excited through the well-defined excitation bands observed in the PLE spectra and energy transfer from the PMMA to the surface passivated CDs. Due to nonradiative processes, which compete with the radiative ones when the temperature is raised, the overall PL intensity of the analysed CDs/PMMA composite was found to slightly decrease with increasing temperature, presenting at RT ∼79% of the intensity recorded at 11 K.

CDs with mean sizes of ∼3.0 nm were embedded in a PMMA host using different loads. The spectroscopic properties of prepared CDs/PMMA composites were evaluated by excitation energy, excitation density and temperature-dependent PL studies. A strong visible emission was observed for all composites, either peaked at the blue or green spectral regions, depending on the used excitation density. The luminescence and excitation luminescence analyses indicate that the recombination is preferentially populated through two main excitation bands located at ∼370 nm and ∼410 nm, which are partially overlapped. Such superposition allows to explain the shift to lower energy of the luminescence band maxima with increasing excitation wavelength. Moreover, the spectroscopic results also indicate that energy transfer occurs between the polymer host and the emitting CDs. As reported by other authors, our data are in good agreement with a model considering an overlapping of emitting centres, which are likely due to small fluorescent molecules at the CDs’ surface. Indeed, temperature-dependent luminescence studies corroborate this assumption, as no shift in the peak position or changes in spectral shape of the main emission were found with increasing temperature. Such behaviour excludes the hypothesis of a possible excitonic-related transition, as observed in a common semiconductor quantum dot, in which a shift in the maximum peak position to lower energies (longer wavelengths) is typically observed due to bandgap shrinkage.
The raw/processed data required to reproduce these findings can be shared upon request.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors acknowledge the financial support from FEDER funds through the COMPETE 2020 Programme and National Funds through Portuguese Science and Technology foundation (FCT) under the project UID/CTM/50025/2013 and POCI-01-0145-FEDER-028755. This work was also developed within the scope of the project CICECO-Aveiro Institute of Materials, POCI-01-0145-FEDER-007679 (FCT Ref. UID/CTM/50011/2013), financed by national funds through the FCT/MEC and when appropriate co-financed by FEDER under the PT2020 Partnership Agreement. Additionally, J. Rodrigues and S. O. Pereira thank I3N for the BPD grants (BPD/UI96/4821/2018 and BPD/UI96/5808/2017) and S. Soreto Teixeira thanks FCT for the grant SFRH/BD/ 05211/2014. The authors also thank to the RNME - Pole University of Aveiro, FCT Project REDE/1509/RME/2005.
